maintainer_org: Microsoft (community-managed going forward; v0.2 line is in maintenance)
execution_kernel_pattern: Event-driven actor-model runtime (autogen-core) underneath a turn-taking team API (autogen-agentchat). The Core layer is explicitly described as an Actor model: agents are not instantiated by user code but are *registered* with the runtime via a factory (`MyAgent.register(runtime, 'my_agent', lambda: MyAgent())`) and lazily created when a message addressed to that agent type arrives. The runtime owns lifecycle, identity, and message delivery — application code never holds a direct reference to a live agent. Two runtime implementations ship: `SingleThreadedAgentRuntime` for in-process applications (controlled with `start()` / `stop_when_idle()` / `close()`) and a gRPC-based distributed runtime (host-servicer + workers) for cross-process and cross-language (Python ↔ .NET) deployments. AgentChat layers a chat-manager / selector loop on top of this substrate.

multi_agent_support.notes: First-class multi-agent. AgentChat ships four team primitives all built on the same shared-context substrate: (1) `RoundRobinGroupChat` — deterministic turn-taking with full broadcast; (2) `SelectorGroupChat` — after every message, an LLM speaker-picker is invoked with a templated prompt containing `{participants}`, `{roles}` (name+description), and `{history}`, and outputs a single agent name; `selector_func` lets user code override or short-circuit the LLM (return a name, or `None` to fall back to the model), `candidate_func` narrows the pool before the LLM decides, and `allow_repeated_speaker` controls whether the same agent may speak back-to-back (default False); (3) `Swarm` — handoff-style coordination via explicit `HandoffMessage`, layered on the group-chat substrate (semantically close to OpenAI Swarm but inside a team); (4) `MagenticOneGroupChat` — research-derived generalist orchestrator with a task ledger + progress ledger that replans on stalls and dispatches to specialist agents (WebSurfer, FileSurfer, Coder, ComputerTerminal). Coordination is mediated by a chat manager / selector rather than peer-to-peer routing; cross-language and distributed teams are possible via the Core runtime. `GraphFlow` was added as a typed-workflow alternative for users who want LangGraph-style explicit state graphs.

memory_model: Per-agent message-history context plus a pluggable `Memory` interface in AgentChat; vector-store backings (e.g., ChromaDB) are provided as extensions but no first-party long-term memory store. Group chats maintain shared broadcast context across the team.
observability_story: Stronger than v0.2 but still thinner than LangSmith/LangGraph. Core ships built-in metric tracking and message tracing as a documented design goal of the v0.4 rewrite; OpenTelemetry hooks are first-party (a dedicated Tracing and Observability tutorial exists). No first-party hosted trace UI — AutoGen Studio surfaces conversation traces interactively for development; production traces are expected to land in OTel-compatible backends.
typical_use_case: Prototyping and building multi-agent applications where several specialized LLM agents (planner, coder, critic, tool-user, human-proxy) collaborate via conversation to solve open-ended tasks: code generation with execute-and-critique loops, web/file research agents (Magentic-One), data-analysis teams, and chemistry/math problem-solving. Also used as a research substrate for studying multi-agent coordination patterns and as a distributed agent runtime for cross-language deployments.
one_paragraph_differentiator: AutoGen's distinguishing bet is the *conversation as universal coordination primitive*, but the v0.4 rewrite reframed how that conversation is implemented: agents are now addressable actors managed by a runtime, not Python objects driven by `initiate_chat()`. Microsoft Research's own v0.4 launch blog says v0.2 users 'struggled with architectural constraints, an inefficient API compounded by rapid growth, and limited debugging and intervention functionality' — the rewrite explicitly targets stronger observability/control, more flexible multi-agent patterns, and reusable components. Mechanically, v0.4 fixes three things v0.2 conflated: (a) agent identity & lifecycle (now runtime-owned), (b) message passing (now async events, not synchronous calls), and (c) topology (now configurable team primitives, not hard-coded ConversableAgent.initiate_chat). The Core runtime supports distributed, cross-language (Python + .NET) deployments — something no major competitor offers natively. Compared to CrewAI it is lower-level and less opinionated about roles; compared to LangGraph it favors emergent conversation over typed state machines (though `GraphFlow` now offers a typed-graph escape hatch); compared to Swarm it is heavier-weight but supports far richer team topologies. Magentic-One on top represents the most ambitious 'generalist agent team' shipped by a major framework.
known_anti_patterns: v0.2 → v0.4 migration churn: synchronous ConversableAgent.initiate_chat idioms are deprecated; many tutorials and Stack Overflow answers reference dead v0.2 APIs
Group-chat coordination is token-expensive — every agent sees every message by default, so naive team scaling explodes context windows
SelectorGroupChat depends on an LLM speaker-picker that can loop or stall; needs careful `TerminationCondition` (TextMentionTermination | MaxMessageTermination) and often a `selector_func` override

Repo is now described as community-managed going forward, signalling reduced direct Microsoft maintenance velocity; Microsoft simultaneously announced AutoGen ↔ Semantic Kernel runtime convergence for early 2025, so the long-term home of the Core runtime is moving
Observability/eval story is improving (first-party OTel hooks) but still has no hosted trace UI on par with LangSmith
